column2: A desktop shows a black screen before the operating system loads and emits a repeating beep pattern. What should a technician do with that beep pattern?
column3: Treat the pattern as evidence that must be matched to the correct system.
column4: Record the exact pattern and check the documentation for that system or BIOS.
column5:
Why

POST beep and code meanings vary by firmware and manufacturer. The strong support response is to capture the evidence and use the correct reference, not to guess from a generic code list.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.48; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield POST Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A PC powers on, never reaches the OS, and shows no video. POST beeps are present. Which troubleshooting direction is most appropriate?
column3: Decide whether the failure happens before or after the operating system loads.
column4: Treat it as a pre-boot hardware or firmware issue, then use the beep pattern and documentation to narrow video, memory, CPU, or BIOS configuration causes.
column5:
Reasoning

The symptom happens before the operating system loads, so starting with Windows repair would be premature. POST evidence helps separate hardware and firmware theories from OS theories.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.48; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic POST Boot Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user says their PC tries to boot from a USB installer they left plugged in. What is the best first fix?
column3: Rule out simple removable-media causes before changing firmware settings.
column4: Remove the bootable media and confirm the BIOS or UEFI boot order if the problem returns.
column5:
Help Desk Logic

Start with the simple, reversible cause. If removable media caused the symptom once, boot order should be checked only if the system keeps selecting the wrong startup device.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.48; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield Boot Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A desktop loses the correct date, time, and firmware settings every time it is unplugged. What is the most likely hardware cause?
column4: A failed motherboard battery.
column5:
Symptom Pattern

The motherboard battery maintains firmware settings while the system is powered off. Replacing it is more targeted than reinstalling the OS or replacing storage.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic CMOS Battery Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A workstation begins blue-screening during startup immediately after a new device driver was installed. What is the best next step before replacing hardware?
column3: Look for the recent change and choose a reversible recovery path first.
column4: Boot into Safe Mode or another recovery path and roll back the driver or use System Restore.
column5:
Troubleshooting Logic

The timing points to the recent driver change. A least-disruptive rollback tests that theory before opening the case or replacing parts.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield BSOD Drivers Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user reports, "The program gave me an error and closed," but they do not know what the error said. What should the technician ask for first?
column3: Improve the quality of the evidence before proposing a fix.
column4: Ask for the exact error text or a screenshot, plus what the user was doing when it appeared.
column5:
Support Logic

Good support starts by improving the quality of the evidence. Exact text, screenshots, and reproduction context are more useful than a vague ticket note.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic CrashScreens Documentation Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user says the computer is running but the monitor is blank. What should you check first before swapping hardware?
column3: Rule out simple external display causes before replacing parts.
column4: Check that the monitor has power.
column5:
Why

Start with the simplest external display cause. A monitor with no power can make a working computer look like it has failed.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield Display Cables Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A desktop has no lights, no fans, and no response when the power button is pressed. What is the first area to verify?
column3: Start with the simplest source of power outside the computer.
column4: Verify that the outlet or power strip is actually providing power.
column5:
Reasoning

No signs of life can be caused before power ever reaches the computer. Testing the outlet or power strip first prevents unnecessary internal hardware work.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield Power Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A PC's case fans spin, but drives and the motherboard do not appear to power up and the system never POSTs. What should you avoid assuming?
column3: One working low-power component does not prove the whole power system is healthy.
column4: Do not assume the power supply is fully healthy just because fans spin. Check power connections and power supply output.
column5:
Exam Trap

Fans can require less power than other devices. Spinning fans are evidence that some power exists, not proof that every required output is correct.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield Power POST Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user says their computer is "slow today." What tool should you check first to narrow whether CPU, memory, or disk activity is high?
column3: Start with a quick way to measure which resource is under pressure.
column4: Task Manager.
column5:
Why

Task Manager gives quick evidence about resource use. That evidence is better than guessing between malware, disk pressure, updates, a bad application, or hardware failure.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield Performance Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A laptop performs normally on AC power but becomes sluggish when running on battery. What setting should you check before replacing hardware?
column3: Compare what changes when the power source changes.
column4: Check for a power-saving mode or policy that throttles performance on battery.
column5:
Support Logic

The symptom changes with the power state. That points toward power management before CPU, memory, or storage replacement.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic Performance Laptop Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A gaming PC shuts down after several minutes of heavy graphics load but works at the desktop. What should you check first?
column3: A problem that appears under sustained load often points to stress or heat.
column4: Check whether the cooling fans are spinning when the system is under load.
column5:
Reasoning

Load-dependent shutdowns often point toward heat. A non-spinning or stalled fan is a concrete cooling fault to confirm before replacing the graphics card or power supply.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield Overheating Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user reports smoke and a burning smell from a desktop. What is the safest first action?
column3: When safety is involved, stop normal troubleshooting before gathering more data.
column4: Disconnect power and stop using the system until the damaged component is found and replaced.
column5:
Safety

Burning odor is not a normal troubleshooting state. Do not keep power applied to "see if it still works."
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield Safety Power Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A workstation randomly powers off with no warning. The user says it happens more often during video rendering. Which evidence should you gather next to test the heat theory?
column3: Use the load pattern to decide which theory needs evidence first.
column4: Check temperature readings during or after the load condition.
column5:
Narrowing

The load pattern makes heat a strong theory. Temperature evidence tests that theory before the technician replaces power or graphics hardware.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic Shutdowns Overheating Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: Only one application closes unexpectedly; the rest of Windows remains stable. What should you check before reinstalling the application?
column3: Capture evidence from the affected application first.
column4: Record the exact application error message.
column5:
Why

Application crashes are narrower than system crashes. The exact message gives a specific symptom to search, document, or escalate instead of treating the whole computer as unstable.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic Applications Logs Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A desktop makes a clicking sound that changes speed when internal fan speed changes. What component should you inspect first?
column3: Match the timing of the noise to the component that changes at the same time.
column4: Inspect the fans for obstruction, bearing problems, or contact with a cable.
column5:
Reasoning

Noise type and timing matter. A clicking sound tied to fan speed is more useful evidence than a broad "replace the computer" conclusion.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic Noises Fans Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: You cannot resolve an intermittent blue-screen issue. What exact user-visible evidence should you include in the escalation ticket?
column3: Capture the evidence the next technician cannot infer later.
column4: Include the exact error text or screenshots.
column5:
Professional Practice

Exact messages and screenshots preserve evidence that may disappear after a restart. They are a concrete handoff item, not a vague "it blue-screened" summary.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield Escalation Documentation Source::Messer-v170

column2: A computer is running, the monitor has power, but the screen is still blank. What signal-path check should you make next?
column3: Follow the display path from the computer to the monitor.
column4: Check that the video cable is connected securely.
column5:
Why

A loose signal cable is a common, reversible cause of a blank monitor. This is a separate retrieval target from checking whether the monitor has power.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic Display Cables Source::Messer-v170

column2: A powered monitor reports that no signal is detected even though the video cable is seated. What monitor setting should you verify?
column4: Verify that the monitor is set to the correct input source.
column5:
Why

A monitor can be connected correctly but listening to the wrong input. Checking input selection is faster and safer than replacing the display or graphics adapter.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic Display Configuration Source::Messer-v170

column2: A workstation powers off during heavy workloads. What log source can help show whether the shutdown was recorded by the operating system?
column3: Use operating-system evidence to separate recorded errors from sudden loss of power.
column4: Event Viewer.
column5:
Why

Event Viewer can preserve shutdown or hardware-related events that help narrow the issue. It does not replace thermal or hardware checks, but it is a specific piece of evidence for escalation.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic Shutdown Logs Source::Messer-v170

column2: Before escalating an intermittent hardware issue, what should you document about each diagnostic step you already tried?
column4: Document the result of each step.
column5:
Professional Practice

Results matter more than a list of attempted actions. The next technician needs to know what each test proved or failed to prove.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic Escalation Documentation Source::Messer-v170

column2: A desktop has no signs of power after being moved. The outlet has already been verified. What should you check next?
column3: Movement often affects removable connections.
column4: Check that the power cable is firmly seated at the computer and power source.
column5:
Reasoning

A loose power cable can make a healthy system appear completely dead. Reseating the cable is a low-risk check before opening the case or replacing parts.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic Power Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A desktop still has no signs of power after you confirm a working outlet and a seated power cable. What power-supply control should you check if the system has one?
column4: Check that the power supply's rear power switch is on.
column5:
Reasoning

Some desktop power supplies have a rear switch that can be turned off during a move or while handling cables. This is still a simple external check before assuming an internal failure.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic Power Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A PC shuts down during heavy load and the fans are spinning. What cooling issue should you visually check next?
column3: Air must be able to move through the system, not just spin a fan.
column4: Check for blocked vents or dust buildup restricting airflow.
column5:
Reasoning

Working fans do not prove the system can remove heat. Blocked vents or dust can still cause load-related shutdowns by preventing airflow through the case or heat sink area.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic Overheating Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: Only one application closes unexpectedly and the user does not remember the error text. What OS evidence source can help find crash details?
column3: Look for a system-maintained record of application failures.
column4: Check Event Viewer for the application crash entry.
column5:
Reasoning

Event Viewer can provide a timestamped record when the user cannot repeat the exact message. That makes the next step more evidence-based than reinstalling immediately.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic Applications Logs Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: An application has crashed several times this week, but the user cannot give exact times. What Windows history view can help spot the pattern?
column4: Check Reliability Monitor for the application failure history.
column5:
Reasoning

Reliability Monitor is useful when the pattern matters more than one exact message. It can show repeated application failures across days and help decide whether the issue follows an update, install, or other change.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column10: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic Applications Logs Scenario Source::Messer-v170
